title: Inverse-designed release-free optomechanical crystal with high photon-phonon coupling
authors: David Hambraeus, Paul Burger, Johan Kolvik, Philippe Tassin, Raphaël Van Laer
abstract: Interactions between light and mechanics provide a powerful interface between optical and microwave-frequency signals, with applications spanning classical signal processing and quantum technologies. High-performance optomechanical devices require both strong photon-phonon coupling and tolerance to parasitic laser heating. Release-free optomechanical crystals provide improved thermal anchoring compared to suspended nanobeams, but have so far exhibited weaker vacuum optomechanical coupling rates, leaving a trade-off between coupling strength and thermal robustness. Here, we largely close this gap: we design and experimentally demonstrate a release-free silicon optomechanical crystal with a record vacuum optomechanical coupling rate of about $g_\text{OM} / (2 π) = 800$ kHz, comparable to suspended state-of-the-art devices. The resulting optomechanical scattering rate $Γ_\text{OM}/(2 π)= 1.1$ kHz is nearly twice that of previous release-free implementations. This performance is achieved by combining physics-guided human intuition with a multiphysics inverse-design algorithm introduced here for resonant optomechanical structures. Beyond the specific device demonstrated, the inverse-design framework is applicable to co-optimizing optical and mechanical resonances and eigenmodes more broadly. These results strengthen release-free optomechanical crystals as a platform for fast, low-noise classical and quantum optomechanics.
summary: The researchers developed a new way to design optomechanical crystals that are attached to their substrate rather than suspended. By using an advanced inverse-design algorithm, they achieved extremely high light-matter coupling rates that rival suspended devices while preventing the device from overheating. This makes it a much more robust platform for quantum technologies.
main_problem: The trade-off in optomechanical crystals between high photon-phonon coupling strength and thermal robustness, where suspended structures provide high coupling but suffer from laser-induced heating.
main_result: The demonstration of a release-free silicon optomechanical crystal with a record vacuum coupling rate of 800 kHz, achieving performance comparable to suspended devices while maintaining superior thermal stability.
method: A combination of the X-HOPE physics-guided technique to match wavevectors and a multiphysics gradient-based inverse-design algorithm using adjoint sensitivity analysis.
model_or_system: A release-free silicon-on-insulator (220 nm Si on 2 um SiO2) optomechanical crystal architecture that is mechanically attached to the substrate to allow for efficient heat dissipation.
key_observables: Vacuum optomechanical coupling rate (g_OM), optomechanical scattering rate (Gamma_OM), optical quality factor (Q_o), and thermo-optic frequency shift.
important_parameters: g_OM/2pi approx 800 kHz, mechanical frequency 5.25 GHz, optical frequency 193.5 THz, and room-temperature operation.
main_assumptions: The design assumes the ability to mitigate fabrication errors through stochastic erosion/dilation and focuses on radiation-limited performance as a benchmark.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows mode overlap and band structure for the X-HOPE design; Figure 2 illustrates the inverse-design algorithm workflow; Figure 3 presents experimental spectral data and coupling measurements; Figure 4 compares thermal stability across different architectures.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the optomechanical coupling/thermal trade-off, introduces the X-HOPE and inverse-design methodologies, details the experimental fabrication and measurement setup, presents the record-high coupling results, and concludes with a robustness analysis against fabrication imperfections.
why_interesting: This work provides a scalable, thermally stable platform for quantum transducers and microwave-to-optical frequency conversion, which are essential for building large-scale quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Release-free optomechanical crystal cavity with improved photon-phonon overlap. (a) The release-free OMC with X-HOPE design pattern showing the transverse component of the normalized electric field Ex of the optical mode (top) and the longitudinal normalized displacement uy of the mechanical mode (bottom). (b) Diagram of unit cell geometry. The basic cell is an elliptical hole with major and minor radii rx and ry. The double cell used for the mirror consists of two holes that have been moved toward each other by a distance s. When s is large, the holes are merged to prevent bridges too thin to fabricate. (c) Optical intensity along the center of the nanobeam for a conventional...

Figure 2. Inverse-design of the optomechanical cavity enhances the optomechanical scattering rate. (a) A diagram depicting the inverse design algorithm. (i) The geometry is imported into the simulation software. (ii) An eigenmode simulation is performed, yielding the eigenmode fields and frequencies. (iii) The gradient of the figure-of-merit w.r.t. the fields is computed. (iv) An adjoint simulation is performed, yielding the adjoint fields. (v) Combining the adjoint and eigenmode fields, a geometric gradient is obtained. (vi) From the geometric gradient, the gradient w.r.t. the design parameters is computed, which in turn is used to obtain an updated set of parameters and the cycle repeats...

Figure 3. Measurement of inverse-designed X-HOPE cavity. (a) Spectral sweep of optical resonance. Lorentzian fit yields internal quality factor of 1.2 · 105, with a total linewidth of κ = 2.37 GHz (b) Scanning electron micrograph of the device and measurement setup featuring a variable optical attenuator, electro-optic modulator, photodetector, spectrum analyzer, and vector network analyzer. (c) Mechanical spectrum for different optical powers, quantified as number of intracavity photons nc. (d) Fitted mechanical linewidth γm as function of nc. The slope of the fitted lines give the vacuum optomechanical coupling rates gOM/(2π) = 770(5) kHz (∆= +ωm) and gOM/(2π) = 835(10) kHz (∆= −ωm)....

Figure 4. Thermo-optic robustness of the X- HOPE cavity. Thermo-optic shift as function of number of intracavity photons nc. The X-HOPE- design from this paper is compared with the conven- tionally designed release-free implementation and a suspended OMC [20, 21].
